section: Class 10 Economics - Chapter 4: Globalisation and the Indian Economy

notes.summary: Globalisation refers to the integration of national economies through increased trade, foreign investment, and rapid flow of information and technology. It connects producers and consumers across countries, creating a global market.
notes.importance: Helps students understand how economic activities are linked worldwide.

notes.summary: MNCs are companies that own or control production in more than one country. They invest through Foreign Direct Investment (FDI), set up factories abroad, and form partnerships with local companies to expand production.
notes.importance: Explains how global production networks function.
quick_facts: Aim to reduce costs and increase profits

notes.summary: Foreign trade involves exports and imports between countries. Trade barriers such as tariffs and quotas are restrictions imposed by governments to control imports and protect domestic industries.
notes.importance: Builds understanding of government policies in global trade.

notes.summary: In 1991, India adopted liberalisation policies by removing trade restrictions and encouraging foreign investment. This increased competition, improved efficiency and integrated India into the global economy.
notes.importance: Highlights a turning point in India’s economic development.

notes.summary: The World Trade Organization (WTO) is an international body that sets rules for global trade and resolves trade disputes between member countries.

Globalisation has benefited consumers by providing greater choice and improved quality of goods. However, small producers and workers in unorganised sectors often face intense competition and job insecurity.
notes.importance: Encourages balanced evaluation of globalisation’s effects.
quick_facts: Growth of IT and service sector
